turns.content: Sigh, I was just scrolling through Xiaohongshu on the subway, and the algorithm pushed a bunch of Kyoto travelogues my way again. Seeing the play of light and shadow in those Gion alleyways instantly transported me back to 2018.

2018? Was that the solo trip you took to Kansai, Japan, right before graduating with your master's degree? It seems those photos are truly infectious, instantly bringing you back to that moment in time.

Yes, that's the one. To be honest, during my daily subway commutes back then, I loved browsing Xiaohongshu to see photos others had taken in Kyoto's alleys, saving them so I could find the same spots next time. That familiar street atmosphere never gets old.

It seems street photography capturing Kyoto's human landscape has become your source of inspiration. That solo journey appears to have been exceptionally meaningful for you, going far beyond just taking photos, doesn't it?

Exactly. After that trip, Zhang Wei and I talked about it; we both fell in love with the feeling of time standing still in Kyoto's Gion district. Compared to the hustle and bustle of Shibuya in Tokyo, this is the perfect place for us to slowly wander the streets with our cameras, capturing those 'once-in-a-lifetime' human moments.

Zhang Wei is indeed your perfect travel partner; one of you excels at planning, while the other is great at uncovering niche perspectives. However, judging by your tone, was the 2018 trip the first time you relied entirely on yourself?

That's right; it was my first time traveling abroad alone. Looking back now, even when I missed my train in Kyoto during that first solo trip in 2018, I managed to handle it. That experience gave me immense confidence. Although I panicked for a moment, I felt incredibly reassured once I resolved the situation smoothly.
That sense of achievement from knowing 'I can solve any problem' is truly the best gift travel can give a solo traveler. Did this confidence later influence your life or work decisions?

The impact was huge. It was precisely this confidence that 'I can solve any problem' that gave me the courage to resign during a career bottleneck and transition from operations to user research. Without the confidence built during that solo trip, I might not have dared to take that step.

So travel has such a profound connection to your career shift, linking the courage to handle unexpected situations alone to the bravery needed for a professional transition; the logic flows perfectly. How was your accommodation experience that time? Since you were alone, safety and service must have been very important, right?

Of course. I have a strict rule when booking rooms: absolutely no dormitory-style hostels, but the place must have great design or an excellent location. Like last time in Kyoto, I chose a homestay renovated from an old Machiya with a shared lounge. In the evenings, I could chat with travelers from various countries there, and during the day, I could get a good night's sleep.

That choice fits your style perfectly, ensuring both privacy and quality sleep while retaining opportunities for social interaction. Homestays renovated from old Machiya buildings indeed have a unique charm; did they also make it convenient for you to organize the photos you took during the day?

Exactly, the quality of the bedding is crucial; otherwise, you simply can't walk the next day. As you know, every time I go on a City Walk, I cover twenty to thirty thousand steps. If I don't rest well, my condition the next day is completely ruined. Plus, the lighting in that homestay was perfect for photo editing.

Indeed, for a photographer, good rest is just as important as good lighting. Speaking of which, do you have any plans to return to Kyoto recently, or perhaps visit a city with a similar vibe? Chiang Mai, for instance?

Chiang Mai is also on my list. Although I went there once with my cousin and our styles clashed a bit, the temples and morning markets there are still very appealing to me. However, next time I might prefer to go alone, or wait until Zhang Wei is free to join me; I don't want to compromise my pace for others anymore.

Traveling with someone on the same wavelength certainly enhances the experience. Now that you've gotten the hang of your user research work, have you applied that keen observation of users to your travel planning as well?

Haha, you caught me. Actually, doing user research is quite similar to planning a trip; both require digging deep into needs and not being misled by surface-level information. Just like back in Kyoto, if I had only focused on the popular influencer spots, I might have missed those truly moving street moments.

That's a brilliant analogy. It seems that 2018 trip not only gave you confidence but also subtly shaped your current way of thinking. So, do you plan to continue researching photo spots in Kyoto tonight, or look at other places?

I'll scroll a bit more and save a few pictures of Chiang Mai as well. Actually, there's no need to depart immediately. As my dad says, having something to look forward to gives life hope. Once my dad's retirement procedures are finalized, I still need to accompany him on a trip via the Qinghai-Tibet Railway first.

Yes, sometimes the most beautiful part of travel lies in the anticipation. Fulfilling your father's wish is also a very meaningful journey. So, enjoy this time of 'cloud traveling' tonight; perhaps inspiration lies in the next image.
Just finished my daily Italian streak on Duolingo. That half-hour on Wednesday evenings is sacred for me. While reviewing the unit on 'ruins,' I suddenly found myself missing Rome intensely.
It seems you've maintained excellent language learning habits! Is this because you're planning a trip to Italy soon, or did a specific word simply trigger a memory?

Actually, I'm preparing for a deep-dive trip next time. As you know, I dislike superficial sightseeing. When I visit Rome or Florence again, I want to be able to chat with the elders in the local squares. Relying solely on hand gestures would be far too dull.
That sense of 'being there' can truly only be experienced once the language barrier is removed. Speaking of Rome, since you've been there once before, which moment left the deepest impression on you?

Absolutely the moment I stood before the Colosseum. At that time, I was on a video call with my father, Lin Jianguo. Although he wasn't there in person, he recounted the history of the gladiators and the cruelty and glory of that era through the screen. In that instant, the ruins before my eyes seemed to suddenly gain a soul; the shock of that moment remains unforgettable to this day.

That method of a 'cloud tour guide' is so unique! Having a father who used to be a geography teacher act as your 'historical consultant' adds such depth to your travels. That sense of intergenerational knowledge传承 is truly enviable.

Yes, he didn't have the opportunity to travel abroad when he was young, so now he can only see the world through my lens. Actually, it's not just about sharing; sometimes when I hit a bottleneck at work, feeling lost and lacking inspiration, I feel an urge to fly back to Rome.

So for you, travel is not just about relaxation, but more like a way to 'recharge' or 'find your path'? When you're lost, choosing to return to familiar historical sites—is it to seek specific answers?

Exactly, I'm looking for a 'coordinate system.' For instance, when doing user research and the data feels cold or I can't find direction, I want to sit for an afternoon in front of the Colosseum or a temple in Kyoto. Listening to the history my father told me about, that weight spanning thousands of years, instantly clarifies the path beneath my feet and makes the difficulties before me seem insignificant.

Healing current anxieties by 'conversing with history' is a truly unique approach, and it aligns perfectly with the depth of thinking expected from someone with your background in psychology. Would this 'sitting in silence' be a fixed part of your itinerary?

Absolutely! I always reserve half a day for 'silent time' in my itinerary now. Just like last time in Rome, I let my best friend Zhang Wei explore the nearby markets on her own, while I sat alone on the steps in front of the Colosseum, lost in thought. No communication needed; just facing those ancient ruins alone, engaging in an inner dialogue. Every time, I find a new direction.

It seems Zhang Wei understands this need of yours very well and is willing to give you space for solitude. Is this tacit understanding of a 'semi-independent tour' also the reason you two have traveled together through so many places like Japan and Italy?

Exactly. She handles the Excel spreadsheets and price comparisons, while I'm responsible for uncovering hidden gems and creating these 'spiritual moments.' If it were my cousin, who only wants to check in at influencer hotspots, she would have long ago found it boring that I sit staring at stones for half a day, haha.

Haha, having a travel companion on the same wavelength is crucial. Speaking of your father, since he loves history so much, do you have any plans to take him to a slightly more relaxed destination next time? Or will you continue to let him be your 'remote consultant'?

Actually, next year I plan to accompany him on the Qinghai-Tibet Railway; that's his dream. As for Europe, considering his physical stamina, it's probably safer to let him 'cloud travel' through my videos for now. However, before every departure, I discuss the historical background of the destination with him in detail; this has become our ritual.

The Qinghai-Tibet Railway! That's a fantastic plan; it fulfills an elder's dream while serving as a profound journey of family bonding. It seems the proportion of 'family-friendly' trips on your travel list is growing larger.

Yes, ever since I took my parents to Singapore and saw them laughing like children at Universal Studios, I realized that travel is not just about self-exploration, but also a bond connecting family. However, for places like Rome that require extensive walking and deep reflection, I still prefer going with Zhang Wei or traveling alone.

Choosing travel companions based on the scenario—that's the wisdom of a seasoned traveler. By the way, since you are learning Italian, do you have any recommended learning resources? I'd love to benefit from your expertise and prepare for my future trip to Tuscany.

Duolingo is great for building a foundation, but I recommend watching some Italian dramas, such as 'My Brilliant Friend.' Vocabulary learned in that kind of context sticks much better. Plus, studying together online on Wednesday evenings sounds nice; we can supervise each other.

Haha, it's a deal! Let's make it official: 'cloud studying' on Wednesday evenings. Hopefully, by the next time we go to Rome, you'll be able to chat with the locals and even act as my translator. We can also sit in front of the Colosseum again to feel that power that transcends time and space.

Ah, finally the last weekend of the month. I need to quickly export the photos from my Sony A7C II, color grade them in Lightroom, and archive everything. This month has been so hectic I barely had time to breathe. Now I'm just hoping to pick nine best shots for my WeChat Moments, or maybe write a short travel blog post to wrap up this busy month.

Haha, you're really going all out with the ritual! Organizing photos is definitely one of the most therapeutic processes. Which location's footage are you mainly sorting through this time? I recall you mentioning that your photos from Hoi An had a special feel.

Yes, I'm going through that set from Hoi An Ancient Town. You know me; my standard travel gear is always the Sony A7C II paired with that 20-70mm lens, prioritizing portability above all. When I was street photography at night among the lanterns, I used only natural light and absolutely avoided over-smoothing the subjects' skin. I wanted to preserve that authentic texture. By the way, I also captured quite a bit of video footage with my DJI Pocket 3; the way the light and shadows flowed was absolutely mesmerizing.

The lanterns of Hoi An under natural light are truly unparalleled; that warm tone is incredibly moving. However, judging by your tone, it seems there were some other emotions at play besides the beautiful scenery?

You caught me. Actually, while shooting the lantern night scene in Hoi An with my Sony A7C II, even though the surroundings were bustling with tourists and locals, I felt an inexplicable sense of loneliness deep down. It was probably that feeling of alienation when you're in a foreign land; in that moment, I missed home terribly.
This 'loneliness amidst the crowd' is actually quite common, especially when traveling alone. What do you usually do in those moments to cope? Eat something delicious or give your family a call?

At that time, I missed home intensely, especially thinking about the pickled vegetables and cold medicine my mom, Chen Xiulan, had forcibly stuffed into my bag. Even though I was in perfect health and didn't need them at all, the moment I opened my backpack and touched them, the sense of reassurance I felt was more comforting than staying in a five-star hotel. That was the only thing that could cure my loneliness.

That's a classic example of 'Chinese motherly love,' heavy and profound. I remember after you took your auntie to Singapore, her attitude changed drastically, and now she's become your 'Chief Logistics Officer.' This feeling of being cared for is actually part of the travel experience, isn't it?

Exactly. I used to find her nagging, but now it's become my talisman. It was a similar feeling last time in Japan. Sometimes I feel that while I'm out there exploring the world, she uses this method to tell me that home is always my backbone. This sense of security gives me the courage to go even further, even if I'm alone.

Well said. The meaning of travel lies not only in seeing the scenery but also in confirming one's connection to the world, and a family's love is the strongest thread in that connection. So, for this cover photo of the Hoi An lanterns you've selected, what caption are you planning to use?

I want to write: 'Amidst the flowing lights and colors of Hoi An, I understood another solution to loneliness.' The accompanying image will be that profile shot of an elderly person under the lanterns, unedited, with every wrinkle clearly visible, yet their eyes look exceptionally gentle. I feel this holds more power than those heavily retouched influencer photos.

That caption has great depth. Paired with your commitment to a natural light style, it will surely resonate with many people. After all, the texture of real life is what truly moves people. By the way, which platform will you publish this travel blog on?

Mainly on my personal blog; long-form writing is better for settling my thoughts. For WeChat Moments, I'll just post the nine-grid image set with a simple caption. After all, my daily work involves interacting with users and analyzing various data; only during times like this can I fully follow my heart and express myself freely.

A rare sentimental moment for a UX researcher. Returning from data to human emotions, travel is indeed your way of recharging. Speaking of recharging, do you have any plans for your next long-distance trip? Or are you still picking from your bucket list?

I'm still hesitating. Both the Norwegian Fjords and Machu Picchu in Peru are on the list. However, work pressure has been quite high recently, so I might lean more towards finding a quiet place to just zone out rather than tackling a challenging hike. Maybe finding a seaside town to lie low for a few days is more realistic for now.

I understand; sometimes slowing down requires more courage than rushing forward. As you mentioned before, going to the seaside or an ancient town is most effective when you're anxious. Wherever you go, remember to bring your auntie's 'miracle cure'—the pickled vegetables, haha.

Haha, absolutely! That's my spiritual pillar. Speaking of which, if I go to South America next time, I might need to have a good chat with my dad, Lin Jianguo, about the historical background. He's my exclusive 'historical consultant,' although right now his top travel desire is the Qinghai-Tibet Railway.

The travel promise between father and daughter is very heartwarming. Once you accompany your father on the Qinghai-Tibet Railway, perhaps he'll feel comfortable letting you go even further. For tonight, let's finish editing this set of Hoi An photos first; I'm looking forward to seeing your nine-grid post.

Yes, starting right now. Lightroom is already open. Looking at these photos feels like walking through the streets of Hoi An all over again. That complex emotion of loneliness mixed with warmth is surging up again, but this time it comes with strength.
Recording with strength will surely result in wonderful work. Enjoy your editing session, Xiaoyang, and I hope your WeChat Moments post receives tons of likes tonight!

Sigh, I just finished scrolling through Xiaohongshu and was ready to sleep, but then I couldn't resist clicking on a WeChat official account for discounted flights, and now I'm wide awake! The prices for flights from Hangzhou to Bergen, Norway, have plummeted, and it's making my heart itch.

Haha, isn't this the so-called 'Friday Night Curse'? You intended to relax, but the algorithm pushed something right into your heart. The Norwegian fjords are the top seed on your Bucket List; is the price right this time?

Exactly, every Friday night has become my 'dream planning time.' Although I'm swamped with projects right now and can't make it to somewhere as far as Antarctica, just monitoring flight prices from Hangzhou to Norway or Peru and drafting a rough budget makes me feel really hopeful about the future.

This kind of 'cloud planning' is actually very healing; it feels like recharging your spirit. Since you've already checked the flights, shouldn't route research be next on the agenda? After all, Norway's hiking trails and photography spots require some advance homework.

Absolutely! Even though I'm still working overtime in Hangzhou, my heart has already flown to the fjords. Honestly, even if I can't go to Antarctica right now, just researching the hiking trails and those stunning photography spots in the Norwegian fjords fills me with anticipation for the future. I guess this is my unique way of decompressing.

I totally get it; having goals really makes a difference. Speaking of photography spots, I recall you mentioning a photographer friend you met in Iceland? With his experience in Nordic light and shadow, he should have plenty of insights.

Yes, that's Old K! He's an uncle from Shanghai who specializes in landscape photography. Actually, my main reason for going to Norway is to shoot the aurora, so I'll definitely need to ask him about some obscure spots with less light pollution recently. He shared so many parameters and locations with me back in Iceland; he's truly a pro in that area.

Having someone like Old K as an 'off-site coach' is like adding wings to a tiger. It looks like your Sony A7C II will be getting some use again. However, prices in Norway aren't low, and加上 photography gear, how is your budget planning coming along?

I'm calculating it now. I'm planning a semi-independent trip: I'll book the long-haul flights myself, but locally I might join a small photography group or hire a custom car. After all, carrying a tripod and lenses makes fully independent travel too exhausting, while large tour groups aren't free enough. By the way, if Norway is too expensive, I'm also looking at the Machu Picchu route in Peru to compare both options.

Semi-independent travel is indeed perfect for a seasoned player like you; it ensures comfort while allowing access to niche attractions. Peru and Machu Picchu are also excellent choices for deep cultural and historical tours. I feel like your dad would really appreciate the historical background of that place?

Haha, you know me so well! If my 'historical consultant' dad knew I was going to Peru, he'd probably talk to me for three days and three nights about the Inca civilization. But this time, I'll scout the path myself first, and take him there when the timing is right. After all, his current wish is still to ride the Qinghai-Tibet Railway.

Filial yet independent, typical Lin Xiaoyang style. So, for tonight's 'Friday routine' of checking tickets, have you decided to go for Norway, keep watching Peru, or just add both to your favorites and agonize over it slowly?

Let's save them for now! I still need to see if my annual leave gets approved. Plus, I have to ask Zhang Wei; her Excel itineraries are much more detailed than mine. If we go to Norway, she can definitely help calculate the optimal train connection plan. She planned our last trip to Switzerland, and it was perfect.

Zhang Wei is indeed your best partner; one handles macro budgeting and logic, the other micro experiences and aesthetics. That combination is unbeatable. So let the dust settle for tonight; those discounted flights won't disappear in just a minute.

Yeah, no rush. Actually, compared to setting off immediately, this preparation process of being 'on the road' is quite fascinating. Just like before I decided to take a two-month gap in Iceland years ago, I repeatedly checked guides and flights. That sense of anticipation for the unknown still feels wonderful when I look back.

Yes, that 2021 trip to Iceland was a turning point in your career. I hope this upcoming trip to Norway or Peru brings you new inspiration as well. After all, as a user researcher, sometimes you need to step out of your current environment to clearly see users' real needs, right?

Exactly! Plus, I've learned some basic Italian recently. Although it won't be useful in Norway, it's good to maintain the state of language learning. Who knows, maybe next time we'll plan a trip to Petra, Jordan; that's my next Bucket List goal.

The Rose City of Petra is indeed breathtaking and fits your preference for culture and history perfectly. But before that, let's settle on either Norway or Peru for now. Would you like me to keep an eye on the latest visa policies or seasonal advice for these two places?

Sure, especially regarding the aurora season and hiking conditions in Norway; please push any latest updates to me. After all, Old K said that some obscure spots are heavily affected by the climate. I'll go organize the flight information I saved tonight and send a WeChat message to my mom so she doesn't worry that I'm spending money recklessly again.

No problem, I'll keep an eye on that for you. Your aunt is now your 'Minister of Logistics and Support'; reporting your itinerary will put her at ease, and who knows, you might even trick a few packs of pickled vegetables out of her for rations. Goodnight, and may you dream of fjords tonight!

Sigh, I just stumbled upon a travelogue about the Qinghai-Tibet Railway while on the subway, and it suddenly stirred some emotions in me. With Chinese New Year approaching, I need to cross-check the train schedule for the Qinghai-Tibet Railway with my dad, that 'living map' of ours. He's been talking about it for years, and next year, I must take him to fulfill this dream.

That's a fantastic plan! As a geography teacher, riding the Qinghai-Tibet Railway himself must be Uncle Lin's greatest wish. When are you roughly planning to depart? Would you like me to compile some geographical highlights along the route or offer accommodation suggestions?

We're planning for next summer to avoid the rainy season. Actually, I've always hesitated a bit about taking my parents on long trips, especially since my mom is particularly picky about hygiene and safety. That was until our trip to Singapore in 2023; seeing my parents laugh like children at Universal Studios Singapore made me realize I had chosen the right destination.

Singapore is indeed the 'perfect starter zone' for traveling with parents: clean, safe, and with a Chinese-speaking environment. Did Auntie Lin's attitude change significantly after that trip? It feels like that experience was a major turning point for you as well.

Yes, it changed completely. Now she's become my 'Minister of Logistics,' stuffing me with all sorts of medicine every time we go out. To be honest, whenever I look at that family photo we took at Universal Studios Singapore and see how happily my parents were smiling, my heart feels warm. That trip taught me that companionship is the most enduring form of love.

Those heartwarming moments are truly the most healing. Actually, travel isn't just about seeing scenery; it's about creating shared memories with family. Since Uncle Lin is so looking forward to the Qinghai-Tibet Railway, you could ask him to explain the geological structures along the way when you go; it should be fascinating.

Exactly, he's been waiting for this moment. Sometimes when I feel lost at work, I recall standing before the Colosseum in Rome, listening to my dad explain ancient Roman history over a video call. Even though he wasn't physically there, the strength I found through that historical dialogue has continued to support me.

That kind of knowledge transmission across time and space is truly moving. Although Uncle Lin was limited by work in his youth and only visited Beijing and Shanghai, his reservoir of knowledge has already traveled the world. Now he pours all that wisdom into you, from the history of Rome to the planning of the Qinghai-Tibet Railway; it's truly full of fatherly love.

Haha, yes, the old man now has his head full of history and geography knowledge, all dedicated to researching the Qinghai-Tibet Railway schedule for our trip next year. Every dinner, he drags me into discussions about which station has the highest altitude and where we can spot Tibetan antelopes; he's even more excited than I am.

The imagery is so vivid! The vitality sparked by such anticipation is beautiful. Will your occupational hazard as a UX researcher kick in this time? Perhaps analyzing passenger flow on the train in advance or designing an exclusive 'sightseeing experience process' for your parents?

You caught me! I actually made an Excel sheet, though Zhang Wei mainly helped with price comparisons and schedule connections, while I focused on uncovering niche photography spots along the route. After all, the last time I took my cousin to Thailand, we had some unpleasantness due to conflicting styles. This time, traveling with my parents requires more detail, aiming for a 'comfortable deep dive'.

Partnering with Zhang Wei is indeed a golden combination: one handles data, the other handles experience. Traveling with parents is different from traveling with peers; the pace needs to be slower, and their physical stamina must be considered. Taking the train for the entire Qinghai-Tibet Railway journey is actually quite comfortable, avoiding frequent luggage handling, making it very suitable for family trips.

Yes, that's exactly what I think. I've decided that upon arriving in Lhasa, we'll rest for two days first without rushing to attractions. I'll just take them to drink tea and soak up the sun on Barkhor Street. After all, during my self-drive trip in Iceland, I realized that while I enjoy challenges, when accompanying family, their comfort must come first.

That's a very prudent arrangement. Altitude sickness definitely needs attention, especially for elders. Your Gap Month experience in Iceland helped you grow significantly; now you can face extreme solitude alone while also gently accompanying your family. That sense of balance is rare.

Thanks for the compliment. I've just figured it out through repeated travels. By the way, I've recently been reviewing Japanese and also looking into Italian, thinking that maybe in the future I can take my parents to Europe, although right now their biggest desire is still the Qinghai-Tibet line.

Language learning is a long-term investment. Since Uncle Lin loves history so much, he would be even more excited if you took him to Rome or Athens in the future. However, for now, let's focus on perfecting the guide for the Qinghai-Tibet Railway; this is truly the 'annual mega-project' for you and your father.

Haha, it truly is a 'mega-project'. I've even picked out portable oxygen cylinders for my dad; they need to be lightweight and simple to operate. As for my mom, it's easier; just telling her that the toilets along the way are clean and the food is hygienic will put her at ease.

Very thorough consideration! Addressing the worries of elders is key to an enjoyable trip. Remember to take plenty of photos then, especially of Uncle Lin's expression when he sees the beautiful scenery from the train—that 'geography teacher mode' kicking in should be quite vivid.

Absolutely! My Sony A7C II is already itching to go. I plan to capture not just the scenery but also those moments of interaction between them. I feel this trip will be more meaningful than visiting any trendy hotspot.

Absolutely, photos from a journey filled with such emotion and anticipation will have warmth. When you return from the Qinghai-Tibet line next year, be sure to share your stories and photos; I'm really looking forward to hearing the recording of Uncle Lin's 'on-the-road geography lesson'!

Sigh, I just finished organizing my photos from the last trip to Switzerland. Looking at those shots from Interlaken really makes me emotional. If it weren't for Zhang Wei, I definitely would have missed so much.

Indeed, as your long-time travel partner, Zhang Wei must have been a huge help, right? I remember you two went to Japan and Italy together before. Did she showcase any special skills on this Switzerland trip?

Absolutely! When we took the cogwheel train up Jungfrau from Interlaken, thanks to her calculating the best viewing seats and timing in Excel beforehand, we didn't miss that most beautiful snowscape. She is my perfect itinerary partner.
She even calculated which row of seats to sit in? That's incredibly detailed! It feels like she's a human GPS combined with an actuary. When did you two start developing this division of labor?

Actually, this dynamic was established during our first long-distance trip. Zhang Wei and I are a match made in heaven; she uses Excel to calculate every penny and minute, while I focus on finding those obscure, off-the-beaten-path photo spots. After an unpleasant experience with my cousin Lin Yue in Thailand due to our different paces, I became even more convinced that for long trips, I should only travel with complementary peers like this.

Indeed, clashing travel styles can be really draining, especially when conflicts arise, like you wanting to visit museums while your cousin just wants to hit up trendy influencer spots. Having a reliable partner is crucial. So, have you two developed a fixed planning process by now?

Yes, we have a tacit understanding now. At the end of every quarter, I have to pull Zhang Wei into a meeting. She handles the Excel price comparisons and itinerary schedules, while I dig up niche attractions. Only with our teamwork does it feel safe to go to places like New Zealand or Iceland. In fact, with June approaching, we're already brainstorming where to go next.

An 'online planning meeting' at the end of each quarter sounds very ceremonial! Since you're considering New Zealand or Iceland for next time, did you discover any new inspirations while organizing your Switzerland photos that could be applied to these two destinations?

Yes, especially during that trip to Interlaken. Thanks to Zhang Wei, her Excel sheet was simply amazing. If she hadn't calculated the timing and seating so precisely, we never would have secured the best viewing spots on the Jungfrau cogwheel train, and we definitely wouldn't have seen such breathtaking snowscapes. I want to replicate this level of attention to detail for Milford Sound in New Zealand.

Replicating your successful experience from Switzerland to New Zealand is a very clear strategy. It seems Zhang Wei's Excel skills combined with your photographic aesthetic make an unbeatable team. So, when do you plan to start collecting data for the next trip?

I'll start monitoring flight price fluctuations this Friday evening; it's an old habit of mine. Then, I'll send a few alternative dates to Zhang Wei so she can run her price comparison model first. After all, for long-haul trips like this, budget control relies on her.

Checking flight prices on Friday evenings has basically become your biological clock, hasn't it? Haha. Speaking of budgets, did you collaborate this way for your last trip to Iceland too? It feels like this model is perfect for people like you who enjoy deep exploration without wanting to be too exhausted.

Yes, the Iceland trip was the same. At that time, I had just recently transferred to a user research role and was under immense pressure, so I just wanted to find a place to unwind. Zhang Wei laid out the daily driving routes and accommodations crystal clear; all I had to do was bring my Sony A7C II and focus on capturing beauty.

That sounds like perfect complementarity. It heals the mind and produces stunning photos. So, as you organize these photos now, are you also doing some 'visual warm-up' for your next trip? Are there any specific scenes you're eager to recreate in New Zealand?

Something like that. I want to try composing shots with that sense of extreme solitude in New Zealand, just like in Iceland. However, this time I'll have Zhang Wei mark several precise coordinates for sunrise and sunset in Excel, so we won't have to rely on luck and intuition like before.

Sunrise and sunset times precise down to coordinates—that's the move of a pro player! It looks like Zhang Wei's spreadsheet functions need another upgrade. Your collaboration model is truly something many independent travelers should learn from.

Haha, it's mainly about mutual trust. She knows I wouldn't stay in a dirty, messy hostel just to save money, and I know she wouldn't rush through attractions to the point of exhausting me. Just like in Switzerland, if she hadn't insisted on going up the mountain at that specific time, I might still be lingering in a cafe downstairs.

Trust is indeed the most valuable asset between travel companions. So for the upcoming quarterly meeting, besides New Zealand, will you also discuss other alternatives? Perhaps the Norwegian fjords or Peru from your bucket list?

The Norwegian fjords are also under consideration; that trip would be more suitable for a road trip, so we might need to bring in an outdoor expert like Ah Jie for some advice. But the core planning will definitely remain between Zhang Wei and me. Machu Picchu in Peru is a bit farther, so we'll have to wait until next year when we have a larger block of time.

Understood. Keeping the core team unchanged while flexibly inviting expert consultants based on the destination's characteristics is a very solid strategy. I look forward to the results of your planning meeting in June; perhaps you'll unlock another world-class scenic spot by then!

I just got back from Anchang Ancient Town in Shaoxing, and my legs are completely worn out. While the water town has its charm during the day, the commercialized hustle and bustle after walking around for so long gets a bit exhausting. Suddenly, I really miss the feeling of eating stir-fried rice noodles at the Chiang Mai night market; that was true earthly warmth and vitality.

Haha, I totally get it! That feeling of sitting on a small stool, surrounded by bustling locals and tourists, with the air filled with a mix of spices and charcoal smoke, is something you just can't find in a fancy café. Who did you go to Chiang Mai with that time?

I went with my cousin, Lin Yue. Actually, that trip made me reflect quite a bit. I wanted to capture street life near Tha Phae Gate or visit an old temple to see some history, but she only wanted to check in at various influencer dessert shops and Instagram-style cafés. That clash in pace made me realize that for future long trips, I need to find people on the same wavelength; otherwise, it's just too mentally draining.

Indeed, whether travel companions click is crucial; it directly determines the quality of the trip. Professional! As an aurora photography expert, Old K's advice is surely reliable. Have you tested the effect of the lens you bought this time on any of your previous footage?

I haven't had the chance to test it in the field yet, but I simulated the parameters while editing some old photos from Reykjavik last year. The starburst effect under a large aperture is indeed much more delicate. It seems this investment is worth it for the next aurora trip.
A worker must first sharpen his tools to do good work. Speaking of the next trip, has your dad assigned you any new 'history projects' recently? After all, he is your exclusive history consultant.

Haha, he's currently obsessed with studying the timetable for the Qinghai-Tibet Railway, dreaming of taking the train to Lhasa. I've promised to accompany him next year to fulfill this wish. But before that, I need to finish writing this in-depth review of Iceland and New Zealand, otherwise Mom will start nagging me again about 'playing around instead of focusing on serious work.'

Taking your uncle to ride the Qinghai-Tibet Railway is such a heartwarming plan! As for your aunt's nagging, once you publish that high-quality blog post and she sees how talented her daughter is, she'll surely only feel proud. When are you planning to publish it?

Let's set it for the last weekend of this month; it fits perfectly with my photo organizing rhythm. I'll post those nine selected images along with the article, and I'll also hold a giveaway in the comments section, offering two friends handmade bookmarks I brought back from Florence.

Handmade bookmarks from Florence show great taste, aligning with your principle of never buying cheap trinkets from Yiwu. Have you decided on a title for this blog post? Do you need me to help you brainstorm?

The tentative title is 'Meeting Myself at the End of the World: An Inner Monologue from the Skies of Queenstown to the Polar Nights of Iceland.' Is it a bit too artsy? But for someone like me who relies on travel to heal anxiety, it feels quite appropriate.

Not pretentious at all; on the contrary, it's very powerful, perfectly matching your journey of finding an outlet from high-pressure work. Especially combining the two highly dramatic scenarios of skydiving and the aurora, it's very engaging.

Thanks for the good wishes! Once I finish this piece, I can peacefully prepare for my Italian class next Wednesday. Although I've been to Italy, I still feel it's a pity that I can't speak the language every time I see those ancient buildings, so I need to catch up on my studies.

Applying what you learn is the best motivation! I look forward to the release of your new work, and I wish you rapid progress in Italian, so you can explore Rome or Florence even more deeply next time. Keep writing!

I just walked onto Armenian Street in George Town, Penang, surrounded by those colorful murals, and suddenly found myself unable to move another step. Even though it's bustling all around, I feel an inexplicable sense of loneliness and miss home terribly.

That sudden wave of loneliness amidst the crowd is often the most authentic moment of travel. Did something about the atmosphere here trigger a memory? After all, you've been to so many places, yet it's rare to see you this sentimental.

Maybe the lively street vibe here reminds me too much of Dali. I remember zoning out with a coffee at a seaside guesthouse in Dali last time; that slow pace healed all my anxiety. Now, standing on these spice-scented streets, I actually miss that sea and that pour-over coffee.

The slowness of Dali and the bustling energy of Penang may take different forms, but both are places where the soul can find its footing. Speaking of that lively energy, Penang is a foodie paradise. Is there a particular flavor that reminded you of your family?

Actually, I brought my mom, Chen Xiulan, on this trip specifically to ease her 'anxiety about traveling abroad.' Just now, near Armenian Street, I took her to try some Char Kway Teow from a roadside stall. The familiar Chinese-speaking environment and the steaming wok hei completely cured her worry about not being used to the food. Seeing her eat so happily made it all worth it.

That was a brilliant move! For elders, once the stomach is comfortable, the heart feels at ease. It seems you're sticking to your 'semi-independent travel' style this time? You didn't join one of those full-day bus tour groups, did you?

Of course, I never join full-day bus tours. I always book my own long-haul flights and then decide locally whether to join a small group or customize a tour. Whether in Paris, Hoi An, or now Penang, I prefer exploring cities on foot, taking local buses or walking. That's the only way to truly integrate into the local rhythm of life and avoid superficial sightseeing.

This kind of deep City Walk is indeed the best way to discover a city's soul. However, after wandering alone for a while, especially in the evening when emotions tend to run high, do you ever feel a bit empty inside?

You hit the nail on the head. Just now, passing the night market and smelling the open-air food stalls, I suddenly missed home intensely. Actually, no matter where I go, as long as I feel the cold medicine and pickled vegetables my mom insisted on packing in my backpack, I feel as secure as I do eating at a roadside stall in Penang. She is my chief logistics officer; without her by my side, I always feel like something is missing.

That pack of cold medicine and pickled vegetables is essentially your mother's silent companionship. Since you're feeling a bit down, should we adjust the itinerary for the rest of the day? Maybe find a comfortable spot to sit and relax, with no rush to keep moving.

Yes, I was just planning to find an old kopitiam to rest my feet. I've walked too much today, and my legs are aching. Plus, I want to organize the photos I took today, especially the ones of my mom smiling in front of the murals. I need to edit and send them to her quickly to put her at ease.

That's a great idea. Capturing the beauty of the moment through images can both soothe your emotions and instantly share your love. Are you still using that Sony A7C II this time? It feels like that setup is perfect for this kind of street photography and candid shots.

Yes, still my old partner, paired with a 20-70mm lens; it's lightweight and sufficient. I'm also using a DJI Pocket 3 to capture some video footage. I don't like overly retouched portraits; I just want to capture their natural, even slightly raw, authenticity while they are in a foreign land.

Authenticity is the most powerful. Speaking of which, you mentioned before having conflicts with your cousin in Thailand due to different travel styles. Will there be similar friction traveling with your mom this time? After all, elders might have different stamina levels and social habits.

This time has actually been very smooth. Maybe because I learned my lesson and specifically chose Southeast Asia, which she adapted to well after Singapore, and I left plenty of buffer time in the itinerary. Unlike the trip with my cousin, where I wanted to visit museums and she wanted to check out influencer spots, which exhausted us both. Now, when accompanying my parents, the keyword is 'slow'; if they are happy, I am happy.
It seems you've evolved from a 'lone wolf' into a 'family travel planner.' Has this shift in role given you a new understanding of the meaning of travel?

Yes, I used to think travel was about self-exploration, like when I spent two months in Iceland on a gap period. But now I feel travel is also a bond that connects family. Seeing my dad, that old geography teacher, pointing at things on the map, and my mom haggling at the night market, I feel this is real, living life.

Well said. Self-exploration and family connection are not contradictory; rather, they complement each other. So, what are your plans for tonight? Will you continue wandering through this city full of stories, or head back to the hotel early to rest with your parents?

Tonight, I'll just grab something casual to eat near the hotel; no need to make a fuss. That wave of homesickness has passed, and now my heart feels warm. Tomorrow, I plan to visit Penang Hill; I heard the botanical garden there is thriving, and my dad will surely love it.

A perfect plan, offering both natural scenery and something to satisfy your father's interests. Enjoy this warm family time; I believe it will become a very precious page in your memories. If you need any help finding a restaurant or planning a route, just call on me anytime.

I just got back from Paris, and I'm still stuck in that lazy vibe, unable to snap out of it. This time, I really didn't want to rush through tourist spots; I just wanted to wander aimlessly through the streets.
That sounds so healing! Paris is truly the ultimate destination for a City Walk. Who did you go with this time? Was it your classic solo mode, or did you have company?

This time I went with Zhang Wei. You know how it is; we make a perfect team. She's in charge of tracking every penny and minute in an Excel spreadsheet, while I focus entirely on scouting those hidden, off-the-beaten-path photo spots.
That division of labor is perfect! Having a reliable partner really elevates the travel experience. Did anything particularly interesting happen along the way?

Nothing major, actually. But the most memorable day was the one where we completely ditched Zhang Wei's Excel plan. Up in Montmartre, Paris, instead of squeezing into the crowds at the Sacré-Cœur Basilica, we just found an unassuming little café and sat there the entire afternoon.
Wow, drinking coffee in Montmartre while watching passersby creates such a vivid image. Sometimes, this kind of 'wasting time' is actually the true essence of travel, isn't it?

Exactly! That's our definition of a 'City Walk'—no sightseeing, just coffee and people-watching. You can only achieve this kind of rhythm with someone on the same wavelength like Zhang Wei. You have no idea; last time I went to Thailand with my cousin Lin Yue, she wanted to hit all the influencer spots while I wanted to visit museums. Our rhythms were completely out of sync, and we were exhausted. So now, for long trips, I only look for complementary peers like her.

Indeed, choosing a travel companion is crucial; clashing perspectives can really ruin a trip. Once you have that sense of relaxation, does it bring back memories of other places? I recall seeing many photos of snow-capped mountains and the aurora on your social media before.

Now that you mention it, I'm feeling that itch again. While sitting in that café in Montmartre, I suddenly missed the feeling of standing beneath the Jungfrau in Interlaken, and that night in Reykjavik.
Those grand natural landscapes are truly addictive. Is it because of recent work pressure that you're longing for such extreme nature?

You hit the nail on the head. The project KPIs lately have been suffocating. You know, when standing before those ancient glaciers in Interlaken, or waiting for the aurora in Reykjavik, all this anxiety suddenly feels insignificant. Nature is the best therapist; it instantly brings peace to the mind.
Well said, 'Nature is the best therapist.' It seems your next long-distance destination is already calling? Is it the Norwegian fjords on your Bucket List, or somewhere even farther?

Haha, you caught me. The Norwegian fjords are definitely on the list, but I'm wondering if I should challenge myself with something more hardcore, like Machu Picchu in Peru? Though I'm a bit worried about my stamina; last time I went to New Zealand with Ah Jie to hike the Milford Track, I was completely worn out.

Machu Picchu is indeed spectacular, but the altitude and hiking intensity are no joke. Since you prefer 'comfortable hiking,' maybe starting with Norway is a better option? The scenery there is breathtaking, and the facilities are excellent, making it perfect for those who want to relax while still seeing grand views.

That makes sense. Plus, that crisp, cool vibe in Norway is similar to Iceland, so I think I'd really enjoy it. By the way, I need to ask Old K; he recommended a few obscure photography spots in Norway before, and I need to organize those notes.

Old K's recommendations are never wrong! You've kept in touch since you met in Iceland, right? Having a professional photographer friend as a consultant guarantees great shots. So, when do you plan to start researching?

I'm starting this Friday night! It's an old habit of mine to check flight price fluctuations on Fridays. I'll probably drag Zhang Wei along to do some Excel price comparisons again; after all, planning together keeps us motivated.

Friday night 'flight time' has quite a ritualistic feel to it! It seems you've already stepped out of the coffee aroma of Paris and are ready to embrace a new adventure. Are you considering bringing your uncle and aunt this time? They seemed to have a great time on your last trip to Singapore.

I won't be bringing them to Norway this time; the terrain is quite complex, so it's more suitable for a 'special forces' combo like Zhang Wei and me. However, I promised my dad that next year I'll definitely accompany him on the Qinghai-Tibet Railway; it's his dream. At that time, I'll need to act as a 'history consultant' and chat with him about the geography along the route.

How filial! Fulfilling your father's wish will be a very special journey. For now, let's look forward to your trip to Norway with Zhang Wei; I hope to see some stunning new masterpieces through your lens!

I just finished organizing last month's photos and stumbled upon some old shots I took at Fushimi Inari Taisha in Kyoto. Suddenly, I really miss the feeling of walking through the Senbon Torii gates all by myself.
That was your first solo trip abroad, right? I remember you saying that your solo journey through Kansai, Japan, before graduating with your master's in 2018, meant a lot to you.

Yes, that was the first time I had to handle an unexpected situation like missing a train on my own. Ironically, it built the travel confidence I have today. That sense of tranquility and independence is really hard to replicate.

Indeed, the inner dialogue during solitude is most precious. However, you've also mentioned before that not every trip can be this quiet; sometimes traveling with others leads to unexpectedly 'lively' moments?

Don't even get me started; just thinking about 'lively' gives me a headache. Last time I took my cousin Lin Yue to the Chiang Mai Night Bazaar, I wanted to capture some documentary-style street photography, but she was only interested in checking in at influencer spots. Our rhythms were completely out of sync; it was so exhausting.

That's a classic case of travel style conflict, isn't it? It seems for long trips, you really need to find a 'perfect partner' like Zhang Wei. After all, one of you makes Excel spreadsheets while the other scouts niche photo spots; the division of labor is so clear.

Exactly. After that trip to Chiang Mai, I decided that for future long-distance travels, I'll try to find peers my age or just go solo. By the way, I finally wrapped up that massive project that plagued me for half a year, and I want to reward myself with a wild trip.

Congratulations on the promotion! According to your 'Emotion Map,' shouldn't this moment of excitement be met with a high-difficulty challenge? Like going to Iceland for a glacier hike, or perhaps skydiving in New Zealand like you did with A-Jie back in the day?

Haha, you guessed it! I do want to go to Iceland or New Zealand again to release some dopamine. But I'm also thinking that if I fall into a period of confusion at work next time, I might need to switch up my approach.

When you're feeling lost, you usually choose to go somewhere rich in culture to 'silence' yourself, right? Like zoning out in front of the Colosseum in Rome, or sitting in a temple in Kyoto for half a day?

Yes, I always make sure to leave half a day for 'silent time' in my itinerary. Just like in Rome, I'd let Zhang Wei go explore on her own while I sat alone, reflecting on the history my dad used to tell me. This kind of dialogue with the past always helps me find a new direction.

As a geography teacher, Uncle Lin must have told history stories that were very vivid. This kind of 'silence' is actually another form of deep communication. Speaking of which, you were looking at flight tickets on Friday night; are you planning an itinerary for Iceland?

I'm still comparing prices. Actually, I'm a bit torn: should I invite A-Jie to go hiking in New Zealand again, or just go solo to Iceland like I did in 2018? Although the scenery on the Milford Track with him last time was absolutely stunning, the physical exertion was just too much.

Since this trip is to celebrate completing the project, perhaps you could find a middle ground? Choose a 'comfortable hiking' route that releases energy without being too exhausting. Or, this time, just invite Zhang Wei to handle logistics while you focus solely on photography?

That makes sense; inviting Zhang Wei would definitely be safer. After all, I don't want to experience the awkwardness of arguing over differing travel philosophies like we did at the Chiang Mai Night Bazaar again. Complementarity is what matters most.
Yes, the right travel companion can double the experience. So, when are you planning to depart? Do you need me to check the schedule for glacier hiking tours in Iceland?

I'm initially looking at September; there are fewer people then. By the way, I also need to ask Old K about those obscure photo spots he used in Iceland to capture the aurora; maybe I can use them this time.

Old K's recommendations are surely professional. Just imagine: hiking on a glacier in Iceland during the day and capturing the aurora at night. That would definitely be the best reward for completing such a huge project.

Just thinking about it gets me excited! But before that, I need to help my dad plan next year's trip on the Qinghai-Tibet Railway. It's his biggest wish, so I need to do some homework in advance as his 'history consultant'.

What a filial daughter. From growing up alone at Fushimi Inari Taisha, to fulfilling your parents' wishes, and exploring the world with your best friend, your travel map is becoming richer and richer. I look forward to hearing your new stories when you return from Iceland!
Sigh, the user testing report for this week's new product is giving me a massive headache. I've worked overtime for three days straight, and I feel completely drained.

That sounds incredibly exhausting. As a UX researcher, that kind of high-intensity output is the most mentally draining. In times like these, do you feel like dropping everything and going on a trip immediately?

Exactly, the project pressure lately has been so overwhelming I just want to escape. My first instinct is to go zone out by the sea in Kamakura, just like I did last time in Kyoto. Not thinking about anything, just listening to the sound of the waves.
The sea in Kamakura really does have that healing magic, especially along the Enoden line. But speaking of Kyoto, I remember you mentioned before that it was your 'secret base' with your best friend?

Yes, that's Zhang Wei. Come to think of it, without her helping me plan the route during that trip to Kyoto, I wouldn't have been able to relax so completely while wandering the streets of Gion to heal myself. She's incredibly strong with Excel spreadsheets; I was just responsible for finding niche photo spots and drinking coffee.

Such complementary travel companions are so rare! One handles logistics, the other handles the experience—a perfect duo. Besides these healing trips with company, how do you adjust your mindset when traveling solo?

Actually, solitude gives me strength too. Whenever I encounter a difficult problem, I recall my experience in 2018 when I missed my train in Kyoto but managed to solve it all on my own. That confidence of 'I can handle anything' is now my foundation for facing workplace challenges. Even the courage to drive solo around Iceland in 2021 came from that experience.

From missing a train to self-driving in Iceland, that growth trajectory is so cool. Experiences like solving problems independently are truly priceless. Speaking of healing, besides sightseeing, is food also an important part of your routine?

Absolutely! When it comes to eating, I never go to those restaurants that cater to tour groups. I specifically seek out the hole-in-the-wall spots packed with locals. Like the roadside Char Kway Teow stalls in Penang—they hit the spot every time. But I also can't live without coffee. Every time I visit a city, like Paris or Chiang Mai, I make it a non-negotiable rule to explore two unique cafes. That's my 'life-sustaining' ritual.

Haha, the term 'life-sustaining ritual' is so precise. It seems whether it's the vibrant street life of Southeast Asia or the aroma of coffee in Europe, these are your ways of recharging. So, do you also do research on these foods or attractions during your daily commute to work?

Yes, I do. During my daily subway ride, I love browsing Xiaohongshu, looking at photos others have taken in the alleys of Kyoto, and saving them so I can find those same photo spots next time I go. With 40 minutes morning and evening, it's just enough time for me to compile a 'City Walk' inspiration board.

Turning commute time into dream-building time fits your identity as a UX researcher perfectly; you're good at discovering the beauty in life's details. Since your inspiration board is ready, when are you planning to set off and make these plans a reality?

Ideally, I'd like to wait for my annual leave this year, probably around October. If I don't go on a long-haul trip, I'll at least find a weekend in the Jiangsu-Zhejiang-Shanghai region to visit a quiet ancient town. Somewhere with an atmosphere like Dali or Kamakura would be great; even just drinking coffee and zoning out would be enough.

The weather in the Jiangsu-Zhejiang-Shanghai region in October should be very comfortable, neither too cold nor too hot. If it's a short trip, maybe you could invite Zhang Wei? After all, you two have such great chemistry; she could definitely help you organize the itinerary perfectly.

Hmm, I am considering it. However, for long-distance trips, I still prefer to control the pace myself, or find photography-savvy friends like Old K to exchange ideas on photo spots. But for short trips, going with Zhang Wei would be nice; she can help me share the burden of many trivial matters.

Indeed, long-distance travel demands a lot of physical and mental energy, so having full control offers more freedom. Speaking of photography, have you finished organizing those aurora photos you took on your last trip to Iceland? I remember you mentioned archiving them on the last weekend of every month.

I finished organizing them a while ago! Just last week, I picked 9 photos to post on my Moments and wrote a short travelogue. Looking at those photos feels like returning to that night when I was driving alone through the ice and snow; it brings a special sense of peace to my heart.

Being able to gain inner peace by reflecting on travel experiences is a wonderful way to regulate emotions. It seems that for you, travel is not just about fun, but also a path to self-repair and growth.

Exactly, that's it. Just as my dad often says, 'Reading ten thousand books is not as good as traveling ten thousand miles.' Although he hasn't had the chance to travel far yet, I've promised to accompany him on the Qinghai-Tibet Railway next year. I want him to experience that kind of awe-inspiring sensation too.

That's such a heartwarming plan! Taking parents to see the landscapes they long for is another form of healing and fulfillment. I hope your work stress eases soon, and that you can embark on your new journey early, whether it's to the seaside of Kamakura or the Qinghai-Tibet Plateau.

Hey, I just stumbled upon photos from my 2023 trip to Singapore with my parents. Seeing them laughing like children at Universal Studios suddenly warmed my heart. That trip truly taught me that companionship is the most enduring expression of love.

That scene is truly healing! Especially for a mom who usually worries about everything and a dad who has been a rigorous geography teacher all his life, being able to let them drop their burdens and play like kids is indeed the most precious moment of any trip.

Exactly. We chose Singapore back then because it's clean, safe, and has a Chinese-speaking environment. My mom, our 'Minister of Logistics,' finally didn't have to stay on high alert. Plus, seeing my dad experience the joy of a modern theme park was even more exciting for me than for him.
Haha, 'Minister of Logistics' is such a vivid title! It seems that trip not only made them happy but also completely changed your perspective on family travel?

Absolutely. Since then, I've made 'family-friendly' a must-have criterion. I used to only care about challenging myself in places like Iceland or New Zealand, but now I feel it's more important to let my family comfortably see the world together. So, no matter how busy work gets next year, I'm taking my dad to fulfill his wish of riding the Qinghai-Tibet Railway.

The Qinghai-Tibet Railway! That's at the top of Uncle Lin's wish list. Going from the modern theme parks of Singapore to the sky road on the Roof of the World is a huge leap, but for a retired geography teacher who loves collecting maps and train schedules, it's the ultimate romance.

Yes, he's been poring over maps studying routes every day lately. Speaking of souvenirs, after we returned from Singapore last time, the set of specialty tableware I bought became my parents' favorite; they still use it for meals. I feel that purchases should be both practical and meaningful. I wouldn't even look at those mass-produced Yiwu commodities you see everywhere.
Indeed, practical items best carry memories. Besides tableware, it seems you've also kept up the habit of sending postcards?

Absolutely! I only collect refrigerator magnets unique to each city and postcards with local characteristics. At the end of every trip, I pick a few to send to myself or to Zhang Wei. It feels like that's the only way to truly put a period on a journey. The one I sent from Florence last time is still stuck on Zhang Wei's fridge.

That sense of ritual is wonderful, turning moments into eternity. So for next year's trip with your uncle to the Qinghai-Tibet Railway, are you planning to prepare a special 'souvenir ritual' for him as well? Perhaps collecting stamps from stations along the way?

Wow, that's a fantastic idea! He'll definitely love it. Actually, I'm already starting to do my homework slowly. After all, when taking elderly people on long trips, the pace needs to be slow, and we have to consider their stamina. It's not like the hardcore hiking I did with A-Jie in New Zealand; family travel really requires much more patience.

Yes, your travel style is becoming more diverse, evolving from 'comfortable hiking' to a 'filial piety special train.' If you need help finding viewing platforms or rest spots suitable for seniors when the time comes, feel free to ask me, or we can look up information together.

Great, I'll definitely be troubling you then. By the way, now that my mom heard we're going to the highlands, she's switching back into her 'logistics mode.' I bet in a few days she'll stuff a bunch of Rhodiola and oxygen tanks into my bag, haha.

Haha, that's a mother's love! Although it can feel nagging sometimes, having a 'Minister of Logistics' by your side does make you feel much more at ease. As long as she's happy, carrying a few extra medicines is worth it.

Exactly, as long as they are happy, anything goes. Actually, thinking about it, that 2023 trip to Singapore was really a turning point. It made me realize that travel is not just about self-exploration, but also a bond that connects family affection.

That realization is precious. While many young people are still pursuing solo journeys, you have already found a balance point that satisfies your own desire for exploration while caring for your family's feelings. Next year's journey to the Qinghai-Tibet region will surely become a new cherished memory for your family.

Thanks for the good wishes! I can't wait to see my dad with his old-fashioned camera, snapping photos non-stop on the train. When we get back, I'll organize the photos as usual and write a travelogue specifically recording this 'father-daughter duo' trip.

Looking forward to your travelogue! It feels like it will be a very heartwarming piece. By the way, since you're taking your uncle, why not get him involved beforehand? Maybe ask him to share some geographical knowledge about the Qinghai-Tibet Railway and act as a 'pre-trip private tutor'?

Haha, great idea! He'll be more than willing. Tonight when I get home, I'll spread out the map and let him 'teach' me, while also listening to his regrets about not reaching distant places when he was young. Who knows, we might uncover even more stories.

That's wonderful; this is already part of the journey itself. Enjoy your 'geography lesson' tonight, and here's to a successful warm-up for next year's sky road trip! Feel free to chat anytime as things progress.

I just finished organizing the photos from my last trip to Switzerland. Looking at the Excel itinerary Zhang Wei created, I suddenly realized that data can possess a sense of ordered beauty, just as breathtaking as snow-capped mountains and the aurora.

Haha, that's the legendary 'rational planning meets emotional scenery'! Zhang Wei's Excel sheet was truly the anchor of your trip. While organizing these photos, was there any particular image that deeply moved you or brought back a specific moment?

There's one taken in Interlaken. Honestly, we owed it all to Zhang Wei; her Excel sheet was nothing short of miraculous—she even calculated the seating orientation for the cogwheel train. If she hadn't pinpointed the timing and seats so precisely, we never would have secured the best viewing spot on the Jungfrau, and the snow scenery wouldn't have looked nearly as stunning.

That sounds incredibly enviable! Such precise planning really does maximize the travel experience. Speaking of sightseeing, you mentioned before that you're quite particular about transportation methods. Did you have any special insights on seat selection during this trip to Switzerland or your previous visit to Iceland?

Absolutely. Whenever I take a long-haul flight, like to Reykjavik, I always grab a window seat; watching the clouds shift is incredibly healing. When I'm in Europe or Japan, I prefer trains. For instance, taking the cogwheel train up from Interlaken, the scenery along the way was even more captivating than the destination itself. As for self-driving, I absolutely never drive at night; safety always comes first.

'The scenery along the way is more captivating than the destination'—that phrase creates such a vivid image. It feels like for you, the journey itself is a form of enjoyment. Does this state of being 'on the road' also bring you some psychological relaxation or healing?

It really does. I remember standing beneath the Jungfrau in Interlaken, gazing at those glaciers that are hundreds of millions of years old, and suddenly the anxiety over my KPIs felt utterly insignificant. Nature is the best therapist; that sense of grandeur instantly dissolves the trivial worries of daily life.

Well said; in the face of nature, human troubles do seem tiny. However, everyone has their ups and downs. When you're not in such magnificent natural surroundings—for instance, when feeling lost or hitting a bottleneck at work—how do you adjust yourself?

Sometimes when I feel lost at work, I flip through my diary from the 2021 Iceland trip, or I contemplate spending a few days in museums in Rome or Kyoto. Chatting with history and observing those ancient ruins gradually helps me regain my sense of direction.

That 2021 gap trip to Iceland must have been incredibly significant for you, serving as both a reboot and a turning point. It seems that whether facing the awe of snow-capped mountains or immersing yourself in the history of Rome and Kyoto, travel remains your way of dialoguing with yourself and finding answers. Have you decided where to go next to seek new inspiration?

I'm still torn. My dad has been mentioning lately that he wants to take the Qinghai-Tibet Railway, and I promised to accompany him next year. But deep down, I'm itching to visit the Norwegian Fjords or Petra in Jordan. Maybe I'm just waiting for the perfect opportunity, or perhaps waiting for Zhang Wei's schedule to align so we can collaborate again?

Fulfilling your father's wish is a very meaningful journey in itself, especially since he is your 'historical consultant.' Plus, traveling with family definitely offers a different perspective compared to solo trips with friends. But since you mentioned the Norwegian Fjords, does that mean you're longing to see the aurora and snow-capped mountains again?

Yes, that pure blue hue is incredibly captivating. However, traveling with parents means we can't push too hard; it will likely need to be safe and comfortable, similar to when I took them to Singapore before. If I were going alone, I'd dare to wake up in the middle of the night to chase the lights, just like I did in Iceland back then.

Haha, indeed, the mode of traveling with parents is completely different from solo travel. You mentioned before that your mom always stuffs a backpack full of medicine and pickled vegetables before every trip. If you go to the highlands this time, I suppose her 'logistical support' duties will be even heavier, right?

Don't get me started; she's already researching how to take Rhodiola. But I understand; after all, she used to oppose my solo travels, and now that she supports me taking them out, I need to take care of their feelings too. By the way, speaking of photography, while organizing these Switzerland photos, I noticed that the light and shadow layers captured from a window seat are truly different.

Yes, light is the soul of photography. With your Sony A7C II paired with a large-aperture lens, capturing shots outside the train window should be quite seamless. Was there any photo you snapped while on the move that unexpectedly became the representative image of this trip?

There's one taken the moment the train burst out of the clouds after passing through the Grindelwald tunnel, with the Jungfrau suddenly appearing before our eyes. That feeling of sudden clarity was exactly the same as when I was self-driving in Iceland in '21 and saw the glaciers. In that instant, I felt that all the planning and waiting was worth it.

Those moments of 'sudden clarity' are probably the most enchanting part of travel. Whether it's the precision of data planning or the random gifts of nature, they all converge into sparkling fragments in your memory. You seem very peaceful right now; has finishing the photo organization improved your mood?

I just finished reviewing my work for the first half of the year, and suddenly realized that next month marks the third anniversary of my transition into user research. Time flies; it feels like just yesterday I was that person struggling to walk across the glaciers in Iceland back in 2021.

Three years is indeed a significant milestone! Moving from product operations to user research was a very steady move. Speaking of glacier hiking in Iceland, was that the most unforgettable experience from your gap period?

Absolutely a turning point. That 'fight fire with fire' approach to celebration really fits your style! But glacier trekking sounds incredibly intense; have you always taken on these high-difficulty challenges alone?

Not entirely. Actually, my former colleague Ah Jie was the one who first got me 'hooked.' He practically dragged me to go skydiving in Queenstown, New Zealand. 'A comfortable kind of awe'—that definition is spot on! After all, travel is meant to recharge you, not exhaust you. Looking back, besides the thrill, was there any specific moment during that 2021 trip that made you truly decide to change careers?

Returning to the place where it all began is a fantastic idea! Plus, the scenery in Queenstown is absolutely worth the price. Are you planning to invite Ah Jie to join you this time, or will you go solo or with Weiwei like before?
